indicator_text: Covered official status as defined by LDA (lobbyist reports any federal government employment in the preceding 20 years)
scoring_rule: hiring 'covered official' lobbyists – those included in the Lobbying Disclosure Act's (LDA) statutory requirement that registered lobbyists report any federal government employment in the preceding 20 years
source_quote: hiring 'covered ofﬁcial' lobbyists – those included in the Lobbying Disclosure Act's (LDA) statutory requirement that registered lobbyists report any federal government employment in the preceding 20 years [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:165-168]
notes: The statutory baseline against which the authors define their measurement contribution. Federal-only (LDA scope). Authors show this is severely under-reported: only 29.7% of their sample self-reported, vs. 51.7% verified — the "transparency loophole" the paper exposes.

indicator_text: Lobbyist's previous federal government employment, verified from biographical sources beyond LDA self-report
scoring_rule: coded as a dummy variable if we verified any previous government experience
source_quote: Table 4 compares lobbyist type and advocacy specializations between conventional and revolving door lobbyists, coded as a dummy variable if we veriﬁed any previous government experience [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:687-689]
notes: Core construct of the paper. Coded 1 if any previous federal government employment was verified via the multi-source search (SOPR, CRP, CQ First Street, Lobbyists.info, LegiStorm, Martindale-Hubbell, LinkedIn, firm websites, etc.) — not just LDA self-report. 51.7% of N=1614 in their sample.

indicator_text: Number of federal government jobs held by the lobbyist
scoring_rule: Sum of distinct federal employing offices identified in biographical search.
source_quote: The 834 revolving door lobbyists in our sample held a total of 1495 federal jobs. These individuals held an average of 1.8 (SD=1.1) jobs ... Nearly half – 47.5 per cent – held more than one federal job [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:533-537]
notes: One of the model predictors. Coefficient 0.058** (Model 1) and 0.037* (Model 2) on clientele diversity. Authors argue raw counts of "covered official" undercount because LDA only requires reporting one prior position; nearly half their RD sample held multiple positions.

indicator_text: Lobbyist was a former elected member of Congress
section_or_category: Branch / position type within revolving door
scoring_rule: 1 if lobbyist served as elected member of Congress; 0 otherwise.
source_quote: there are only 12 former members in the lobbyist sample, so there is insufﬁcient variation in their clientele diversity and the standard error is high by comparison [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:891-892]
notes: Model predictor. Distinguished from staff because of normative concerns and because they are media salient but rare (12 of 1614 = 0.74%). Null effect on clientele diversity but authors caution this reflects rarity not lack of effect.

indicator_text: Lobbyist worked as congressional staff (House or Senate, personal/committee/leadership office)
section_or_category: Branch / position type within revolving door
scoring_rule: 1 if lobbyist worked in Congress in any non-member capacity (personal office, committee, or leadership).
source_quote: the most common type of revolving door lobbyists are those who worked for a House member, although a signiﬁcant portion worked in multiple jobs and in more signiﬁcant positions on and off Capitol Hill [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:644-647]
notes: 75% of revolving-door lobbyists in sample worked in Congress (623/834). Underlies the staff-hierarchy scale below.

indicator_text: Congressional staff hierarchy scale (climbing the ladder from member's personal office only to member+committee+leadership)
source_quote: we constructed a scale variable that ranges from the relatively low level of cases of staffers whose employers were unidentiﬁable or were in a legislative support service agency to a high level where an individual staffer held jobs in member, committee and party leadership ofﬁces [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:634-637]
notes: Model predictor (b=0.020*, Model 1). The headline finding: well-connected congressional staffers have more economically diverse clienteles, consistent with the access-peddling view. 7-level scale (Table 3).

indicator_text: Lobbyist worked in the White House (executive office of the President)
section_or_category: Branch / position type within revolving door
scoring_rule: 1 if lobbyist held a White House position; 0 otherwise.
source_quote: Former White House employees were 55 per cent Republican and 34 per cent Democrat, which makes sense as our sample was drawn in the eighth year of the George W. Bush administration [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:589-591]
notes: Model predictor; null effect on clientele diversity. 87 of 834 RD lobbyists. Distinguished from federal bureaucracy because political appointees rather than career civil servants.

indicator_text: Lobbyist worked in a federal bureaucracy / executive-branch agency
section_or_category: Branch / position type within revolving door
scoring_rule: 1 if lobbyist worked in a federal bureaucracy (career civil service or non-WH political appointee).
source_quote: executive branch bureaucrats-turned-lobbyists retain a more economically concentrated clientele than other lobbyists [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:848-849]
notes: Model predictor. 223 of 834 RD lobbyists. Negative coefficient (-0.083 in Model 1; not significant in Model 2): bureaucrats-turned-lobbyists retain MORE concentrated clienteles, consistent with industry-specific expertise rather than access-peddling.

indicator_text: Lobbyist worked in the federal judiciary
section_or_category: Branch / position type within revolving door
scoring_rule: 1 if lobbyist held a federal judicial-branch position; 0 otherwise.
source_quote: Typically, these jobs were as judicial clerks, which does not match the intuitive deﬁnition of the revolving door. We report them here, but exclude them from subsequent analyses. [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:1096-1097]
notes: Reported descriptively (28 of 834) but excluded from regression analyses (typically clerkships, do not match intuitive RD definition).

indicator_text: Political party of the lobbyist's former government employer (Democrat / Nonpartisan-Unknown / Republican)
section_or_category: Branch / position type within revolving door
scoring_rule: Coded by party of the office-holder for whom the lobbyist worked.
source_quote: We code lobbyists' former employers' political party as Democrat, Nonpartisan/Unknown or Republican [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:585-586]
notes: Used as a face-validity check, not as a regression predictor. Patterns: Congress 43% Dem / 43% Rep; White House 34% Dem / 55% Rep (sample drawn in 8th year of Bush admin); bureaucracy/judiciary mostly nonpartisan.

scoring_rule: 0 = in-house lobbyist and 1 = contract lobbyist — coded from CRP lobbying database.
source_quote: we identiﬁed contract lobbyists in CRP's lobbying database and coded 0=in-house lobbyist and 1=contract lobbyist. Revolving door lobbyists are more likely to work as hired consultants than conventional lobbyists by a ratio of nearly 2:1. [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:690-693]
notes: Control variable. Strongest single predictor of clientele diversity in Model 1 (b=0.550**). RD lobbyists 50% contract vs. 30% for conventional (Table 4). Used as left-censoring criterion in Model 2.

indicator_text: Lobbyist works at a large general-interest firm (vs. boutique / special-interest firm)
scoring_rule: 0 = small, special-interest boutique firms ... 1 = general interest firms; coded from firm websites, CRP "Top Lobbying Firms" list, Lobbyists.info, Lexis-Nexis Martindale-Hubbell.
source_quote: we distinguish between small, special-interest boutique ﬁrms that explicitly promote themselves as focusing on one or a few industries or issues = 0 and general interest ﬁrms = 1 [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:693-695]
notes: Control variable. Subset of contract lobbyists. RD = 85% / conventional = 84% (no significant difference). Coefficient 0.107* in Model 1.

indicator_text: Advocacy specialization: legislative policy and process
scoring_rule: 1 if lobbyist focuses on policy issues in Congress.
source_quote: we code lobbyists as legislative policy and process experts = 1 if they focus on policy issues in Congress. Empirically, this specialization is extremely common; 87 per cent of the sample specializes in legislative lobbying [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:706-708]
notes: Control. Near-universal (87%) so weak discriminator. Null effect in regression.

indicator_text: Advocacy specialization: federal budget and appropriations / earmarks
scoring_rule: coded as 1 if they reported lobbying on 'Federal Budget and Appropriations' for any client in any quarter in 2008
source_quote: funding earmark specialists – coded as 1 if they reported lobbying on 'Federal Budget and Appropriations' for any client in any quarter in 2008 – we ﬁnd that revolving door lobbyists differ from their conventional counterparts. Revolving door lobbyists are 27 per cent more likely to do appropriations lobbying [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:715-718]
notes: Control variable; one of the most diagnostic. RD = 48% / conventional = 38% (z=4.26*). Coefficient 0.184** in Model 1: appropriations lobbying strongly predicts clientele diversity, consistent with access-peddling.

scoring_rule: 1 if lobbyist specializes in legal and regulatory matters (amicus briefs, notice-and-comment letters, etc.).
source_quote: preparing highly technical amicus briefs or notice-and-comment letters for proposed regulations requires a greater deal of speciﬁc legal and policy expertise than run-of-the-mill legislative monitoring or appropriations earmarking [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:326-329]
notes: Control. RD = 30% / conventional = 31% (no significant difference). Negative coefficient (-0.053*, Model 1): regulatory specialists have MORE concentrated clienteles, consistent with industry-specific expertise.

indicator_text: Number of LDA clients per lobbyist
scoring_rule: Count of distinct clients reported on LD-2 reports for the lobbyist in 2008.
source_quote: Lobbyists in our sample represented just over four clients from two economic sectors on average in 2008 [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:732-734]
notes: Input to clientele-diversity construction. Mean 4.2, SD 8.1, range 1-93 (Table 5). Counted from 51,475 quarterly LD-2 reports in 2008.

indicator_text: Number of distinct CRP economic sectors represented in lobbyist's client portfolio
scoring_rule: Count of distinct sectors among lobbyist's clients (CRP codes clients into 13 economic sectors).
source_quote: CRP codes all lobbying clients by their primary economic mission into 13-sector categories such as agribusiness, transportation, health and ﬁnancial services [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:727-729]
notes: Input to clientele-diversity construction. Mean 2.1, SD 2.2, range 1-13 (Table 5). Sectors are CRP's 13-category economic-mission classification of registered clients.

indicator_text: Clientele diversity (normalized Shannon's H entropy across the 13 CRP economic sectors of a lobbyist's clients)
scoring_rule: Normalized Shannon's H: -sum p(xi)*ln p(xi) / ln(N), where p(xi) is proportion of clients from sector i and N is total sectors.
source_quote: we calculate a normalized Shannon's H entropy score as a universal measure for clientele economic sector diversity [papers/text/LaPira_Thomas_2014__revolving_door_lobbyists.txt:765-766]
notes: The paper's headline outcome variable. Theoretical low ≈ 0 (single sector), high ≈ 1 (uniform across all 13 sectors). Mean 0.13, SD 0.22, range ~0 to 0.88 (Table 5). Treated as left-censored in Tobit/censored-regression models. Empirical inference: low diversity = expertise-driven; high diversity = access-driven.
